professor.name: Dr. Diaz
professor.text: For this week, we've been exploring how museums handle objects that were taken from other countries long ago. Several major museums have recently begun returning artifacts to their countries of origin, while others argue that these pieces reach a far wider audience where they currently sit. Should museums be required to return artifacts to the cultures they came from? Why or why not?

students.name: Claire
students.text: I believe museums should return these artifacts. An object carved for a specific community carries meaning that a foreign display case tends to strip away, and the people it belongs to often have no realistic way to see it otherwise. Sending it home respects that origin instead of treating another culture's heritage as property.

students.name: Andrew
students.text: In my opinion, keeping many of these pieces in large museums does more good overall. Millions of visitors who could never afford to travel abroad get to encounter them firsthand, and well-funded institutions have the expertise to preserve fragile objects that might otherwise deteriorate in a less-equipped local facility.

professor.name: Dr. Achebe
professor.text: As we discussed last week, coding has shifted from a narrow specialist skill to something woven into a huge range of careers. Some educators argue that every student should be required to learn programming in order to stay competitive, while others say that time would be better spent on broader reasoning that doesn't go out of date. Should programming be a required subject for all students? Why or why not?

students.name: Paul
students.text: I think programming should be required for everyone. Even students who never become developers already work alongside software every day, and understanding how it is built helps them judge what is realistic and what isn't. Basic coding also teaches a logical, step-by-step way of thinking that transfers to almost any field.

students.name: Kelly
students.text: In my opinion, pushing every student into programming crowds out subjects that matter just as much. Not everyone thinks naturally in code, and a semester spent wrestling with syntax could instead build writing or reasoning skills that stay useful no matter how quickly the tools themselves change.
